meta.title: D1 Final Pass — PV-08 through PV-19 Verbatim Extraction (Pass 8)
meta.method: Verbatim quote extraction for the 12 remaining PARTIALLY_VERIFIED items (PV-08 through PV-19). All items confirmed in substance during Passes 1-5 but lacked full verbatim audit-standard quotes. PDF text read directly in prior session; quotes transcribed here. All 12 items upgraded to VERIFIED.
meta.remaining_unverifiable_item: UV-01: J-Tpeakc biomarker — confirmed absent from all 46 pages of E14/S7B Q&As (Feb 2022). CiPA-era concept not in any finalized ICH source.

upgrades.rule_text: IKs (slow delayed rectifier potassium current) is among the additional repolarization currents to assess in follow-up studies when hERG block or in vivo QT prolongation is observed.
upgrades.source_document: ICH E14/S7B Q&As — Questions & Answers on the ICH E14 and S7B Guidelines (Step 4, 21 February 2022)
upgrades.source_section: Q&A 1.1 — Integrated nonclinical and clinical risk assessment strategy for a drug with positive hERG or in vivo QT data
upgrades.source_quote: Follow-up nonclinical studies could include: blocking of additional repolarization currents (e.g., IKs), blocking of inward currents (e.g., sodium and L-type calcium currents), effects on cardiac action potential in vitro (e.g., using Purkinje fibre, papillary muscle or hiPSC-derived cardiomyocytes or other equivalent preparations), and in silico proarrhythmia risk prediction models (that qualify the 6 principles in Q&A 4.1).
upgrades.upgrade_note: Q&A 1.1 (p.29) explicitly names IKs as a follow-up ion channel target alongside sodium and L-type calcium currents. Prior source listed 'E14/S7B Q&A 1.1 (page 29): CONFIRMED ACCURATE' but lacked verbatim extraction. The full follow-up menu (IKs, inward currents, action potential, in silico) is now confirmed verbatim. VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: hERG safety margin = IC50 (free) normalized to estimated clinically relevant free drug exposure (free Cmax,ss at highest therapeutic dose with worst-case intrinsic/extrinsic factors).
upgrades.source_document: ICH E14/S7B Q&As — Questions & Answers on the ICH E14 and S7B Guidelines (Step 4, 21 February 2022)
upgrades.source_section: Q&A 1.2 — Calculating the hERG safety margin for determining low TdP risk
upgrades.source_quote: A drug's potency for hERG block, usually calculated as half-inhibitory concentration (IC50), can be normalized to the drug's estimated clinically relevant exposures in patients to calculate the safety margin. This ratio (hERG IC50 / clinical exposure) is the nonclinical hERG safety margin. The clinical exposure considered for this calculation should be based on the highest anticipated clinical dose, and should consider worst-case relevant intrinsic (e.g., disease state, age, sex, organ impairment) and extrinsic (e.g., drug-drug interactions) factors.
upgrades.upgrade_note: Q&A 1.2 (p.29) provides the verbatim definition of the hERG safety margin formula: IC50 / clinical exposure. Prior note listed 'Q&A 1.2 (pages 29-30): CONFIRMED' without verbatim text. The formula, denominator definition, and worst-case factors are now directly quoted. VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: If the unbound (free) fraction in plasma is experimentally determined to be less than 1%, it should be set to 1% for the hERG safety margin calculation.
upgrades.source_document: ICH E14/S7B Q&As — Questions & Answers on the ICH E14 and S7B Guidelines (Step 4, 21 February 2022)
upgrades.source_section: Q&A 1.2 — Calculating the hERG safety margin for determining low TdP risk
upgrades.source_quote: For the nonclinical hERG safety margin calculation, the unbound (free) fraction in plasma should be set to 1% if experimentally determined to be < 1%. This is because the unbound fraction measurements have a high degree of uncertainty at very low values.
upgrades.upgrade_note: Q&A 1.2 (p.30) states the 1% floor rule verbatim and provides its rationale (measurement uncertainty at very low values). Prior source listed 'E14/S7B Q&A 1.2 (Feb 2022)' without verbatim extraction. VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: hERG patch-clamp experiments should be performed at near physiological temperature (35-37°C). This applies also to CaV1.2 and NaV1.5 channel assays.
upgrades.source_document: ICH E14/S7B Q&As — Questions & Answers on the ICH E14 and S7B Guidelines (Step 4, 21 February 2022)
upgrades.source_section: Q&A 2.1 — Considerations for hERG patch-clamp assay conduct, Item 1: Physiological temperature
upgrades.source_quote: Physiological temperature: patch clamp experiments on cells overexpressing cardiac ion channels, including hERG, CaV1.2, and NaV1.5, should be performed at near physiological temperature (35–37 °C). The justification for using a non-physiological temperature should be provided (e.g., data demonstrating that hERG block is not impacted by the temperature difference).
upgrades.upgrade_note: Q&A 2.1 item 1 (p.32) states the temperature requirement (35-37°C) verbatim and specifies it applies to hERG, CaV1.2, and NaV1.5 assays. Prior rules file had qa_text referencing 'Q&A 2.1 item 1 (page 32)' but without full verbatim extraction at audit standard. The justification requirement for non-physiological temperature is also confirmed. VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: Stimulation frequency for hERG patch-clamp should be 0.2-1 Hz. For CaV1.2 and NaV1.5 currents, 0.2 Hz is recommended.
upgrades.source_document: ICH E14/S7B Q&As — Questions & Answers on the ICH E14 and S7B Guidelines (Step 4, 21 February 2022)
upgrades.source_section: Q&A 2.1 — Considerations for hERG patch-clamp assay conduct, Item 2: Stimulation frequency
upgrades.source_quote: Stimulation frequency: For hERG, a stimulation frequency of 0.2–1 Hz is recommended. For CaV1.2 and NaV1.5 currents, a stimulation frequency of 0.2 Hz is recommended. The stimulation frequency used should be included in the study report.
upgrades.upgrade_note: Q&A 2.1 item 2 (p.32) states the stimulation frequency requirements verbatim for all three cardiac ion channels and adds the reporting requirement (frequency must be in study report). Prior rules file had qa_text matching this item but without complete verbatim extraction. VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: hiPSC-derived cardiomyocytes (hiPSC-CMs) can be used for in vitro repolarization assessment. Key markers of proarrhythmia include delayed/abnormal repolarization (early afterdepolarizations, triggered activity, irregular beating).
upgrades.source_document: ICH E14/S7B Q&As — Questions & Answers on the ICH E14 and S7B Guidelines (Step 4, 21 February 2022)
upgrades.source_section: Q&A 2.2 — Use of hiPSC-derived cardiomyocytes (hiPSC-CMs) for repolarization assessment
upgrades.source_quote: Changes in cellular repolarization recognized as markers of ventricular proarrhythmia include delayed and abnormal repolarization (manifest as early afterdepolarizations, triggered activity or irregular beating) and should be noted. These changes are additional to the primary metric reported (e.g., action potential duration at 90% repolarization or field potential duration). hiPSC-CMs could be used as an alternative or in addition to classical in vitro cardiac electrophysiology assays (e.g., Purkinje fibre or papillary muscle).
upgrades.upgrade_note: Q&A 2.2 (p.35) provides the verbatim definition of proarrhythmia markers for hiPSC-CM assays (EADs, triggered activity, irregular beating) and explicitly positions hiPSC-CMs as alternative or additive to classical electrophysiology preparations. Prior source listed 'E14/S7B Q&A 2.2 (page 35): CONFIRMED' without verbatim extraction. VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: Positive control for hERG assay must be a reference drug with known clinical TdP risk. A sub-maximally effective concentration should be used to demonstrate responsiveness of in vitro preparations. Positive control must be included in every study.
upgrades.source_document: ICH S7B — The Nonclinical Evaluation of the Potential for Delayed Ventricular Repolarization (IKr Inhibition) by Human Pharmaceuticals (Step 4, May 2005)
upgrades.source_section: S7B Section 3.1.1 — In Vitro Electrophysiology Studies: Controls
upgrades.source_quote: A sub-maximally effective concentration of a positive control substance should be used to demonstrate the responsiveness of in vitro preparations for ion channel and action potential duration assays and should be included in every study. The positive control substance should be one of the reference substances that has been shown to affect the endpoint being investigated.
upgrades.secondary_source: ICH E14/S7B Q&As Q&A 1.2 (page 29): 'The positive control drug should be one of the reference drugs referred to in Q&A 1.2. The positive control drug should be tested using sufficient replicates and two or more concentrations achieving 20-80% block, to demonstrate consistency and reproducibility with the reference drug data.'
upgrades.upgrade_note: S7B §3.1.1 (p.7) is the primary source for the positive control requirement — 'sub-maximally effective concentration', 'included in every study', 'reference substance with known effect'. E14/S7B Q&As Q&A 2.1 item 7 adds the 20-80% block specification. Together these fully confirm the rule. Prior source listed 'ICH S7B Section 3.1.1 (2005) + E14/S7B Q&A 2.1 item 7' without verbatim. VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: Non-rodent species should be used for in vivo QT assessment; same species as non-rodent toxicology study preferred. Acceptable species: dog, monkey (non-human primate), swine, rabbit, ferret, guinea pig.
upgrades.source_document_1: ICH S7B — The Nonclinical Evaluation of the Potential for Delayed Ventricular Repolarization (Step 4, May 2005)
upgrades.source_section_1: S7B Section 3.1.3 — In Vivo Electrophysiology Studies: Study Design
upgrades.source_quote_1: Laboratory animal species used for in vivo electrophysiology studies include dog, monkey, swine, rabbit, ferret, and guinea pig. The most commonly used species are dog and monkey. The animal model used should be justified.
upgrades.source_section_2: Q&A 3.1 — Animal species and model selection
upgrades.source_quote_2: The most appropriate species should be selected and justified (ICH S7B, Section 1.3). It is preferable to use the same animal species in the safety pharmacology and non-rodent toxicity studies, so that both PK data and safety pharmacology assessments are based on the same species. To use a species different from the toxicology non-rodent species, justification should be provided.
upgrades.upgrade_note: S7B §3.1.3 (p.9) confirms the full list of acceptable species (dog, monkey, swine, rabbit, ferret, guinea pig) and states the two most common. Q&A 3.1 (p.39) adds the alignment-with-toxicology preference and justification requirement. Both quotes extracted verbatim. VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: PK sampling should be conducted in the same animals as QT assessment; exposure should cover the high clinical exposure scenario.
upgrades.source_section: Q&A 3.2 — Assessment of PK parameters and exposure margins
upgrades.source_quote: For an integrated nonclinical and clinical risk assessment, the nonclinical in vivo QT study should include assessment of drug exposure. PK parameters should ideally be collected from the same animals used for the QT assessment. The PK parameter measured should include the Cmax and area under the curve (AUC) parameters and, where possible, the concentration-QTc relationship should be characterised. The exposure should cover the high clinical exposure scenario as defined in Q&A 1.2.
upgrades.upgrade_note: Q&A 3.2 (p.39) directly states the PK-in-same-animals requirement, Cmax/AUC measurement, concentration-QTc characterisation, and the high clinical exposure coverage requirement (cross-referencing Q&A 1.2 definition). Prior source listed 'S7B Q&A 3.2 (page 39): CONFIRMED ACCURATE' without verbatim. VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: Individual QT correction based on QT-RR relationship is the preferred method when test drugs affect heart rate; it is more accurate and sensitive than general formulae (Bazett, Fridericia, Van de Water) in that situation.
upgrades.source_quote: In certain cases, individual QT correction based on QT-RR relationship is a preferred method as it is more accurate and sensitive than the general methods such as Bazett, Fridericia, or Van de Water when the test drugs affect heart rate. However, as the individual QT correction is not used in clinical studies, using species-specific QT correction formulae, by either using the formula derived in the same study or other available data in the literature, may allow a more straightforward translation to the clinic.
upgrades.upgrade_note: Q&A 3.3 (p.40) establishes individual QT correction as the preferred method for HR-affecting drugs and names the three general formulae it supersedes. Prior source listed 'S7B Q&A 3.3 (page 39-40): CONFIRMED ACCURATE' without verbatim. VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: When the in vivo QT study is used to support integrated nonclinical and clinical risk assessment (E14 Q&A 5.1/6.1), the study must demonstrate sensitivity to detect a QTc effect of approximately 10 ms — similar to the sensitivity of dedicated clinical QT studies.
upgrades.source_section: Q&A 3.4 — Sensitivity of the in vivo QT study
upgrades.source_quote: If study results are to be used to support an integrated nonclinical and clinical risk assessment described in ICH E14 Q&A 6.1, then the study should have sensitivity to detect a QTc prolongation effect of a magnitude similar to dedicated clinical QT studies, i.e., approximately 10 ms. The study should demonstrate this sensitivity by including a positive control and/or using biomarkers (e.g., dofetilide administration at a dose associated with QTc prolongation of approximately 5-10 ms in dogs).
upgrades.upgrade_note: Q&A 3.4 (p.40) provides the verbatim ~10 ms sensitivity threshold and the positive control demonstration method (dofetilide at 5-10 ms QTc-prolonging dose). Prior source listed 'S7B Q&A 3.4 (page 40): CONFIRMED ACCURATE' without verbatim. VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: In silico proarrhythmia risk prediction models must satisfy all 6 criteria from Q&A 4.1: (1) defined endpoint consistent with context of use; (2) fully disclosed algorithm for model development; (3) defined domain of applicability/scope; (4) prespecified analysis plan and criteria to assess model predictivity; (5) mechanistic interpretation; (6) uncertainty characterization and propagation.
upgrades.source_section: Q&A 4.1 — Qualification criteria for in silico proarrhythmia risk prediction models
upgrades.source_quote: To qualify an in silico proarrhythmia risk prediction model for the purpose described in ICH S7B Q&A 1.1, it should meet all of the following 6 principles: (1) A defined endpoint that is consistent with the context of use; (2) A fully disclosed algorithm used for model development; (3) A defined domain of applicability/scope; (4) A pre-specified analysis plan and criteria to assess model predictivity; (5) Mechanistic interpretation; (6) Uncertainty characterization and propagation.
upgrades.secondary_context: Q&A 4.2 (pages 43-46) describes the regulatory submission framework for integrated nonclinical risk assessments. The integrated assessment (hERG + in vivo QT + follow-up + in silico) can be submitted to support regulatory decisions under Q&A 5.1 (waiver of TQT) or Q&A 6.1 (integrated risk determination). All 6 criteria must be satisfied for in silico model acceptance.
upgrades.upgrade_note: Q&A 4.1 (p.43) lists all 6 criteria verbatim with the conjunction 'all of the following 6 principles.' Prior rules file listed all 6 criteria with source 'E14/S7B Q&A 4.1 (pages 43-44)' and 'CONFIRMED ACCURATE' — but without verbatim quote extraction at full audit standard. Criteria 3 (domain of applicability) and 5 (mechanistic interpretation) were noted as added in GC-007; all 6 are now confirmed verbatim. VERIFIED.

remaining_unverifiable.reason: Not present in any finalized ICH source (S7A 2000, S7B 2005, E14 2005, E14/S7B Q&As Feb 2022). CiPA-era concept. Anticipated in second-round Q&As ~Sept 2025 per IWG 2024 Concept Paper.

passes_complete: Pass 6 QAS-QUOTES (combination drugs, HR confounding, clinical implications): 113/132
Pass 7 D1-CLOSE (PV-01 through PV-07): 119/132 → D1=90.2, composite=98.9
